facts.text: Caddyshack (1980) was largely improvised — director Harold Ramis encouraged Bill Murray, Chevy Chase, and Rodney Dangerfield to ad-lib most of their scenes.
The gopher puppet in Caddyshack was added late in production to tie the loosely connected storylines together.
Rodney Dangerfield's famous line 'Hey, everybody, we're all gonna get laid!' in Caddyshack was completely improvised.
Bill Murray's entire Carl Spackler monologue about the Dalai Lama in Caddyshack was improvised on the spot.
Caddyshack was a box office disappointment on release but became one of the best-selling home video comedies of all time.
Airplane! (1980) was based on a 1957 film called Zero Hour!, and the writers bought the remake rights for $2,500.
The makers of Airplane! specifically cast serious dramatic actors like Robert Stack, Leslie Nielsen, and Lloyd Bridges to make the jokes funnier.
Leslie Nielsen made over 100 films before Airplane! reinvented him as a comedic actor at age 54.
Airplane! contains an average of three jokes per minute, one of the highest joke densities in film history.
The 'Don't call me Shirley' line from Airplane! was voted the funniest movie quote of all time by multiple polls.
Blazing Saddles (1974) was nearly shelved by Warner Bros. executives who thought it was too offensive to release.
Mel Brooks wrote Blazing Saddles with Richard Pryor, who was originally cast as the lead before the studio deemed him too controversial.
The campfire beans scene in Blazing Saddles was the first time flatulence was heard in a major motion picture.
Blazing Saddles broke the fourth wall in its climax, with the characters literally crashing through the studio wall into other film sets.
Animal House (1978) was made for just $2.8 million and grossed over $141 million, making it one of the most profitable comedies ever.
John Belushi had very few scripted lines in Animal House — most of his performance as Bluto was improvised physical comedy.
Animal House inspired the real-life tradition of toga parties on college campuses across America.
The food fight scene in Animal House took an entire day to film and used real cafeteria food.
Ghostbusters (1984) was originally written by Dan Aykroyd as a futuristic sci-fi epic set in space with dozens of Ghostbuster teams.
The Ghostbusters theme by Ray Parker Jr. reached No. 1 on the Billboard Hot 100 and stayed there for three weeks.
Bill Murray agreed to do Ghostbusters only on the condition that Columbia Pictures would fund a film adaptation of The Razor's Edge.
The Stay Puft Marshmallow Man costume in Ghostbusters weighed over 75 pounds and took several puppeteers to operate.
Ghostbusters held the record for the highest-grossing comedy film for several years, earning $295 million worldwide.
Slimer in Ghostbusters was nicknamed 'Onionhead' during production and was designed as a tribute to John Belushi.
Bridesmaids (2011) grossed over $288 million worldwide, proving that female-led R-rated comedies could be massive hits.
The food poisoning scene in Bridesmaids was filmed at a real bridal shop in Los Angeles, and the owner almost refused to allow the shoot.
Melissa McCarthy's role in Bridesmaids was originally much smaller, but Judd Apatow insisted on expanding it after seeing her audition.
Kristen Wiig co-wrote Bridesmaids with Annie Mumolo while both were still performing at the Groundlings improv theater.
The Hangover (2009) was made for $35 million and grossed over $467 million worldwide, becoming the highest-grossing R-rated comedy at the time.
The Hangover was inspired by a real trip to Las Vegas that writer Jon Lucas took with friends.
Zach Galifianakis's portrayal of Alan in The Hangover was largely based on a character he developed in his stand-up act.
The tiger in The Hangover was real, and Mike Tyson's cameo was filmed in just one day.
The photos during the end credits of The Hangover were actually taken during filming to maintain continuity.
Superbad (2007) was written by Seth Rogen and Evan Goldberg when they were just 13 years old.
The character names Seth and Evan in Superbad are the real first names of writers Seth Rogen and Evan Goldberg.
Michael Cera improvised many of his awkward interactions in Superbad, including much of his dialogue with Jonah Hill.
Step Brothers (2008) was largely improvised — Will Ferrell and John C. Reilly did so many takes that the DVD included an alternate version called the 'Unrated' cut with completely different jokes.
The 'Did we just become best friends?' scene in Step Brothers was improvised by Will Ferrell and John C. Reilly.
Anchorman: The Legend of Ron Burgundy (2004) had so much extra footage that a second film, Wake Up, Ron Burgundy, was assembled from deleted scenes.
The jazz flute scene in Anchorman was inspired by Will Ferrell's real ability to play the flute.
The news team street fight in Anchorman featured uncredited cameos from Ben Stiller, Luke Wilson, Vince Vaughn, and Tim Robbins.
Will Ferrell stayed in character as Ron Burgundy during the entire press tour for Anchorman 2 in 2013.
Jim Carrey was paid $350,000 for Ace Ventura: Pet Detective (1994) and $20 million for The Cable Guy (1996) just two years later.
Jim Carrey was the first actor to earn $20 million for a single film, which he received for The Cable Guy.
The Mask (1994) used groundbreaking CGI to bring Jim Carrey's elastic facial expressions to even more exaggerated life.
Dumb and Dumber (1994) was rejected by every major studio before New Line Cinema agreed to produce it.
Jim Carrey chipped his front tooth for real for his role in Dumb and Dumber — he simply had a cap removed that had been covering a childhood injury.
Liar Liar (1997) was one of the few Jim Carrey comedies based on an original script rather than adapted from existing material.
Bruce Almighty (2003) grossed $484 million worldwide, making it Jim Carrey's highest-grossing film.
Jim Carrey turned down $10 million to do a sequel to Elf because he felt the script wasn't good enough — the role went to no one, as Elf 2 was never made.
In Ace Ventura: Pet Detective, Jim Carrey's 'talking butt' scene was improvised and almost cut from the film.
Adam Sandler's films have collectively grossed over $6 billion worldwide, making him one of the most commercially successful comedic actors in history.
Happy Gilmore (1996) was inspired by Adam Sandler's real love of hockey — the slapshot-style golf swing was his idea.
Billy Madison (1995) was heavily panned by critics but became a cult classic, grossing $26 million against a $10 million budget.
The Wedding Singer (1998) was set in 1985 and featured over 30 songs from the 1980s in its soundtrack.
Adam Sandler signed a four-movie deal with Netflix in 2014 worth a reported $250 million, later extended for more films.
50 First Dates (2004) was filmed entirely on location in Hawaii, one of Sandler's favorite filming destinations.
Uncut Gems (2019) showed Adam Sandler's dramatic range and earned him widespread critical acclaim despite no Oscar nomination.
Click (2006) was one of the rare Adam Sandler comedies that made audiences cry, blending comedy with genuine emotional depth.
Will Ferrell got his big break on Saturday Night Live in 1995 and stayed for seven seasons before leaving in 2002.
Elf (2003) was almost made with Jim Carrey in the lead, but Will Ferrell's earnest performance made Buddy the Elf iconic.
Elf grossed over $220 million worldwide and has become one of the most-watched holiday films of all time.
Talladega Nights: The Ballad of Ricky Bobby (2006) had entire scenes that were completely improvised by Will Ferrell and John C. Reilly.
Will Ferrell co-founded the comedy website Funny or Die in 2007 with Adam McKay, launching it with the viral 'The Landlord' video.
The Other Guys (2010) reunited Will Ferrell with director Adam McKay and featured an unusually action-heavy comedy plot.
Blades of Glory (2007) required Will Ferrell and Jon Heder to train with professional figure skaters for months.
Mel Brooks is one of the few entertainers to have won an Emmy, Grammy, Oscar, and Tony (an EGOT).
Mel Brooks's The Producers (1967) was his directorial debut and won him the Academy Award for Best Original Screenplay.
Young Frankenstein (1974) was shot entirely in black and white to mimic the look of the original Universal monster films.
Mel Brooks used the same laboratory props from the original 1931 Frankenstein film in Young Frankenstein.
Spaceballs (1987) was Mel Brooks's parody of Star Wars, and George Lucas gave his blessing on the condition that no Spaceballs merchandise be sold.
Robin Hood: Men in Tights (1993) was Mel Brooks's response to Robin Hood: Prince of Thieves, which he found unintentionally funny.
History of the World, Part I (1981) ended with a joke trailer for Part II — which didn't actually get made until a Hulu series in 2023.
Seinfeld aired its final episode on May 14, 1998, drawing 76.3 million viewers — one of the most-watched TV finales ever.
Seinfeld was famously pitched as 'a show about nothing,' though creators Larry David and Jerry Seinfeld always disputed that description.
The character of George Costanza on Seinfeld was based on co-creator Larry David's real personality and life experiences.
Jerry Seinfeld turned down $5 million per episode to continue Seinfeld for a tenth season, which would have been over $100 million.
Seinfeld's 'The Contest' episode about a bet to see who could go the longest without masturbating won the Emmy for Outstanding Individual Achievement in Writing.
The Seinfeld episode 'The Chinese Restaurant' was almost rejected by NBC because the entire episode took place in one location with no main plot.
Kramer's physical comedy on Seinfeld was inspired by Michael Richards's background in physical theater and his admiration for Buster Keaton.
The Office (US) premiered on March 24, 2005, and initially received mixed reviews before becoming one of the most beloved sitcoms in TV history.
Steve Carell was not the first choice for Michael Scott on The Office — the role was originally offered to several other actors including Paul Giamatti.
The Office used a documentary-style single-camera format that was revolutionary for American network sitcoms at the time.
Jim and Pam's wedding episode of The Office drew 8.2 million viewers, the show's highest-rated episode.
Rainn Wilson based his portrayal of Dwight Schrute partly on the original UK character Gareth Keenan and partly on his own eccentric relatives.
The 'Dinner Party' episode of The Office is frequently cited by fans and critics as one of the greatest sitcom episodes ever made.
John Krasinski wore a wig during part of Season 3 of The Office because he had shaved his head for the film Leatherheads.
Parks and Recreation premiered on April 9, 2009, as a spinoff concept of The Office but quickly developed its own identity.
Amy Poehler's portrayal of Leslie Knope in Parks and Recreation was initially written as more incompetent but evolved into an enthusiastic, hyper-competent public servant.
Nick Offerman built the canoe that Ron Swanson crafts in Parks and Recreation — he is a real-life master woodworker.
Chris Pratt improvised the line 'I typed your symptoms into the thing up here and it says you could have network connectivity problems' in Parks and Rec.
The fictional town of Pawnee in Parks and Recreation was inspired by various small towns in Indiana.
Rob Lowe and Adam Scott joined Parks and Recreation in Season 2, which many fans credit as the turning point that saved the show.
Curb Your Enthusiasm premiered on October 15, 2000, and ran for 12 seasons over 24 years.
Curb Your Enthusiasm has no script — each episode is based on a detailed outline, and all dialogue is improvised by the actors.
Larry David created Seinfeld with Jerry Seinfeld before creating Curb Your Enthusiasm as a semi-autobiographical show about his own life.
Larry David left Seinfeld after Season 7 but returned to write the series finale, which was controversially received.
The 'Seinfeld reunion' storyline in Curb Your Enthusiasm Season 7 served as the unofficial Seinfeld reunion that fans had wanted for years.
It's Always Sunny in Philadelphia premiered on August 4, 2005, and holds the record for the longest-running live-action comedy series in American TV history.
The pilot of It's Always Sunny in Philadelphia was shot on a home video camera for about $200.
Danny DeVito joined the cast of It's Always Sunny in Philadelphia in Season 2, which helped the show gain a wider audience on FX.
The Gang in It's Always Sunny are intentionally written as terrible people — the writers call it a show about 'the worst people in America.'
Arrested Development premiered on November 2, 2003, and was canceled by Fox after three seasons despite critical acclaim and a devoted fanbase.
Arrested Development's narrator, Ron Howard, was also the show's executive producer and occasionally appeared on screen.
Arrested Development pioneered the dense, layered joke style where gags set up in early episodes paid off seasons later.
Netflix revived Arrested Development for a fourth and fifth season in 2013 and 2018, making it one of the first major TV revivals by a streaming service.
The Bluth family frozen banana stand in Arrested Development was inspired by real boardwalk banana stands in Southern California.
Eddie Murphy's Delirious (1983) stand-up special is considered one of the greatest comedy specials ever recorded.
Eddie Murphy was just 19 years old when he joined the cast of Saturday Night Live in 1980, becoming its youngest cast member at the time.
Eddie Murphy's Beverly Hills Cop (1984) was originally written as a dramatic action film for Sylvester Stallone.
Coming to America (1988) grossed $288 million worldwide and spawned a sequel 33 years later.
Eddie Murphy played multiple characters in The Nutty Professor (1996), spending up to eight hours in makeup for some roles.
Eddie Murphy was paid $15 million for his voice role in Shrek (2001), contributing to the film's $484 million worldwide gross.
Dave Chappelle walked away from a $50 million deal with Comedy Central in 2005, citing creative and ethical concerns about Chappelle's Show.
Chappelle's Show premiered on January 22, 2003, and became Comedy Central's highest-rated program during its run.
Dave Chappelle's 'Rick James' sketch became one of the most quoted comedy sketches of the 2000s.
Dave Chappelle won his first Grammy for Best Comedy Album in 2018 for The Age of Spin & Deep in the Heart of Texas.
Kevin Hart's 'Let Me Explain' (2013) comedy concert film grossed $32 million, one of the highest-grossing stand-up films ever.
Kevin Hart voices Snowball the rabbit in The Secret Life of Pets franchise, which has grossed over $1.2 billion combined.
Kevin Hart sold out Lincoln Financial Field in Philadelphia in 2015 with over 50,000 attendees — one of the largest stand-up comedy shows ever.
Chris Rock's 'Bring the Pain' (1996) HBO special is consistently ranked among the top five stand-up specials of all time.
Chris Rock hosted the Academy Awards three times, in 2005, 2016, and 2022.
Chris Rock's comedic style was heavily influenced by Eddie Murphy and Sam Kinison.
Judd Apatow's directorial debut was The 40-Year-Old Virgin (2005), which grossed $177 million on a $26 million budget.
The chest-waxing scene in The 40-Year-Old Virgin was done for real — Steve Carell's screams and reactions were genuine.
Judd Apatow produced or directed many defining comedies of the 2000s, including Knocked Up, Superbad, Bridesmaids, and Pineapple Express.
Knocked Up (2007) launched Seth Rogen into leading-man status and grossed $219 million worldwide.
Judd Apatow's Freaks and Geeks (1999-2000) launched the careers of Seth Rogen, James Franco, and Jason Segel despite lasting only one season.
Pineapple Express (2008) combined stoner comedy with action-movie tropes, earning $101 million worldwide.
This Is 40 (2012) was a 'sort-of sequel' to Knocked Up, following the supporting characters played by Paul Rudd and Leslie Mann.
Trainwreck (2015) was written by and starred Amy Schumer, with Judd Apatow directing.
Charlie Chaplin's The Kid (1921) was the first feature-length comedy film and one of the highest-grossing films of the silent era.
Charlie Chaplin was one of the co-founders of United Artists in 1919, giving him full creative control over his films.
Charlie Chaplin's The Great Dictator (1940) was his first full talking picture and a bold satire of Adolf Hitler.
Chaplin was so popular that by 1916, there were Charlie Chaplin look-alike contests held across the United States.

Buster Keaton performed all his own stunts, including a scene in Steamboat Bill, Jr. (1928) where a real house facade fell around him — he stood in the exact spot where the window opening would pass over him.
Buster Keaton earned the nickname 'The Great Stone Face' because he never smiled on camera during his films.
The General (1926), Buster Keaton's Civil War comedy, was a commercial failure on release but is now considered one of the greatest films ever made.
Buster Keaton broke his neck during the filming of Sherlock Jr. (1924) and didn't realize it until years later.
The Three Stooges made 190 short films for Columbia Pictures between 1934 and 1959, one of the longest runs in film history.
The Three Stooges' physical comedy — eye pokes, slaps, and bonks — was carefully choreographed and enhanced with sound effects.
Curly Howard of the Three Stooges shaved his head for the role, sacrificing his full head of hair for the act.
Laurel and Hardy made over 100 films together between 1927 and 1951, spanning silent films and talkies.
Stan Laurel was the creative force behind Laurel and Hardy, writing gags and directing many of their films uncredited.
Oliver Hardy weighed over 300 pounds but was an accomplished dancer and athlete in real life.
Saturday Night Live premiered on October 11, 1975, with George Carlin as host and musical guests Janis Ian and Billy Preston.
The original SNL cast — the 'Not Ready for Prime Time Players' — included Dan Aykroyd, John Belushi, Chevy Chase, Jane Curtin, Garrett Morris, Laraine Newman, and Gilda Radner.
SNL has launched the careers of countless comedians, including Eddie Murphy, Mike Myers, Tina Fey, Will Ferrell, Amy Poehler, and Adam Sandler.
Tina Fey became SNL's first female head writer in 1999 and later co-anchored Weekend Update with Jimmy Fallon.
The 'More Cowbell' sketch with Will Ferrell and Christopher Walken is one of the most iconic SNL sketches of all time.
SNL's Weekend Update segment has been running since the show's first episode and has had over 20 different anchors.
Chevy Chase was the first cast member to leave SNL, departing after just one season to pursue a film career.
SNL is broadcast live from Studio 8H at 30 Rockefeller Plaza in New York City, where it has been filmed since 1975.
Eddie Murphy's stint on SNL from 1980 to 1984 is credited with saving the show from cancellation during a ratings slump.
The Second City improv theater opened in Chicago on December 16, 1959, and has trained generations of comedy legends.
Second City alumni include Bill Murray, John Belushi, Dan Aykroyd, Gilda Radner, Tina Fey, Amy Poehler, Steve Carell, and Stephen Colbert.
The Upright Citizens Brigade Theatre (UCB) was co-founded by Amy Poehler, Matt Besser, Ian Roberts, and Matt Walsh in 1999.
UCB's improv style, based on long-form improvisation called 'The Harold,' influenced an entire generation of comedy writers and performers.
Groundlings, the LA-based improv school, trained Will Ferrell, Melissa McCarthy, Kristen Wiig, Maya Rudolph, and Phil Hartman.
The comedy duo of Abbott and Costello popularized the 'Who's on First?' routine, which is in the Baseball Hall of Fame.
Dean Martin and Jerry Lewis were one of the biggest comedy duos of the 1950s, making 16 films together before their bitter split in 1956.
The Blues Brothers (1980) started as an SNL sketch and became a feature film starring John Belushi and Dan Aykroyd that destroyed over 100 cars.
Wayne's World (1992) began as an SNL sketch and grossed $183 million, spawning the catchphrases 'Party on!' and 'Schwing!'
Key and Peele's sketch show ran for five seasons on Comedy Central and won a Peabody Award for excellence in comedy.
Mike Myers drew on his Canadian upbringing for many of his characters, including Wayne Campbell and Austin Powers.
The original Austin Powers (1997) was a modest hit, but its sequel The Spy Who Shagged Me (1999) opened to $54 million.
Monty Python's Life of Brian (1979) was banned in several countries for its religious satire and partly funded by George Harrison of the Beatles.
Monty Python and the Holy Grail (1975) was made on a shoestring budget — coconut halves replaced horses because they couldn't afford real ones.
The Spanish Inquisition sketch is one of Monty Python's most famous bits: 'Nobody expects the Spanish Inquisition!'
Monty Python's Flying Circus ran for just 45 episodes on the BBC from 1969 to 1974 but changed comedy forever.
Some Like It Hot (1959) starring Marilyn Monroe, Tony Curtis, and Jack Lemmon is often ranked as the greatest comedy film of all time.
Dr. Strangelove (1964) was originally written as a serious thriller, but Stanley Kubrick found the material so absurd that he turned it into a dark comedy.
Peter Sellers played three different roles in Dr. Strangelove and was originally cast in a fourth before an injury prevented it.
The Big Lebowski (1998) was a box office disappointment but became one of the biggest cult comedies of all time, inspiring an annual festival called Lebowski Fest.
Jeff Bridges based much of his portrayal of The Dude in The Big Lebowski on real-life film producer Jeff Dowd.
Office Space (1999) flopped in theaters but became a massive cult hit on DVD and cable, inspiring countless workplace memes.
The red Swingline stapler in Office Space didn't exist as a real product until after the film — Swingline later manufactured it due to popular demand.
Napoleon Dynamite (2004) was made for $400,000 and grossed over $46 million, one of the most profitable independent comedies ever.
Borat (2006) was largely filmed with real, unsuspecting people who didn't know Sacha Baron Cohen was performing a character.
Sacha Baron Cohen stayed in character as Borat for entire press junkets and interviews to promote the film.
Mean Girls (2004) was written by Tina Fey and based on the non-fiction book Queen Bees and Wannabes by Rosalind Wiseman.
The line 'On Wednesdays, we wear pink' from Mean Girls became one of the most quoted movie lines of the 2000s.
Mean Girls was released on April 30, 2004, and has since been adapted into a hit Broadway musical.
Groundhog Day (1993) was directed by Harold Ramis and is now considered one of the greatest comedies ever, though it received only modest reviews on release.
Bill Murray reportedly lived the time loop of Groundhog Day for roughly 10 years according to director Harold Ramis's original estimate, though later analyses suggest it could be even longer.
Mrs. Doubtfire (1993) required Robin Williams to spend four and a half hours in makeup each day to transform into the elderly nanny.
Robin Williams improvised so much during Mrs. Doubtfire that the film had enough alternate takes to be re-edited into an R-rated cut.
Good Morning, Vietnam (1987) earned Robin Williams his second Oscar nomination — much of his radio DJ dialogue was improvised.
Robin Williams was classically trained at Juilliard, where his roommate was Christopher Reeve.
There's Something About Mary (1998) grossed $369 million worldwide and made the Farrelly Brothers the kings of gross-out comedy.
American Pie (1999) launched a franchise of nine films (four theatrical, five direct-to-video) and grossed over $989 million combined.
The 'pie scene' in American Pie was shot in one take — Jason Biggs's reaction was partly genuine embarrassment.
Legally Blonde (2001) was initially dismissed as a fluffy comedy but became a feminist cult classic and grossed $141 million worldwide.
Reese Witherspoon studied with real Harvard Law students to prepare for her role as Elle Woods in Legally Blonde.
Dodgeball: A True Underdog Story (2004) features Ben Stiller's character White Goodman, whose look was designed as a parody of fitness infomercial hosts.
The fake ESPN8 'The Ocho' network from Dodgeball became so popular that ESPN actually created a real 'The Ocho' programming block.
Zoolander (2001) was released just weeks after 9/11 and underperformed at the box office, but became a massive cult hit on home video.
Ben Stiller's 'Blue Steel' look in Zoolander was based on poses he noticed male models making at fashion shows.
Old School (2003) was an early collaboration between director Todd Phillips, Will Ferrell, Vince Vaughn, and Luke Wilson.
Will Ferrell's 'Frank the Tank' streaking scene in Old School was filmed on an actual public street.
Wedding Crashers (2005) grossed $285 million worldwide and revitalized both Vince Vaughn's and Owen Wilson's careers.
The Nutty Professor (1996) remake was a vehicle for Eddie Murphy to play seven different characters using extensive prosthetic makeup.
Tropic Thunder (2008) featured Robert Downey Jr. controversially playing an actor in blackface — the film satirized Hollywood's insensitivity rather than endorsing it.
Tom Cruise's unrecognizable cameo as Les Grossman in Tropic Thunder was kept secret until the film's release.
Shaun of the Dead (2004) was the first film in Edgar Wright's 'Cornetto Trilogy,' followed by Hot Fuzz and The World's End.
Hot Fuzz (2007) required Simon Pegg and Edgar Wright to watch over 100 action films for research.
The Naked Gun: From the Files of Police Squad! (1988) was based on a short-lived TV series that lasted only six episodes.
Leslie Nielsen appeared in over 100 dramatic films before his deadpan comedic roles in Airplane! and The Naked Gun.
Ferris Bueller's Day Off (1986) was written by John Hughes in just six days.
The parade scene in Ferris Bueller's Day Off was filmed during a real parade in Chicago — many of the crowd reactions were genuine.
Matthew Broderick addressed the camera directly in Ferris Bueller's Day Off, breaking the fourth wall throughout the film.
John Hughes wrote and directed a string of iconic 1980s comedies including Sixteen Candles, The Breakfast Club, and Planes, Trains and Automobiles.
Planes, Trains and Automobiles (1987) features Steve Martin and John Candy in what many consider the greatest comedy road movie ever made.
John Candy improvised the emotional monologue at the end of Planes, Trains and Automobiles that reveals his character's loneliness.
National Lampoon's Vacation (1983) was written by John Hughes and based on his short story 'Vacation '58' published in National Lampoon magazine.
Beverly Hills Cop (1984) was the highest-grossing film of 1984, earning $316 million worldwide.
Coming to America (1988) was partly filmed at a real McDonald's in Queens, New York, which Paramount converted into the fictional McDowell's.
Trading Places (1983) paired Eddie Murphy with Dan Aykroyd and was a modern retelling of Mark Twain's The Prince and the Pauper.
Bill Murray's performance in Lost in Translation (2003) showed his dramatic range and earned him an Oscar nomination for Best Actor.
Rushmore (1998) began Bill Murray's long collaboration with Wes Anderson, which continued through The Royal Tenenbaums, The Life Aquatic, and many more.
What About Bob? (1991) created real tension between Bill Murray and Richard Dreyfuss, who reportedly didn't get along during filming.
Stripes (1981) was one of Bill Murray's first leading roles and helped establish him as a box office draw.
The Blues Brothers (1980) featured musical performances by James Brown, Aretha Franklin, Ray Charles, and Cab Calloway.
John Belushi passed away on March 5, 1982, at age 33, cutting short one of the most promising comedy careers in history.
The term 'bromance' in comedy was popularized by films like I Love You, Man (2009) and Superbad.
Mel Brooks and Carl Reiner performed their '2000 Year Old Man' routine for friends at parties for years before recording it as an album in 1960.
Get Out (2017) blended horror with sharp social comedy and won Jordan Peele the Oscar for Best Original Screenplay.
Jordan Peele was one half of the sketch duo Key and Peele before becoming an acclaimed horror-comedy director.
The Jerk (1979) was Steve Martin's first starring film role and contained the famous line 'I was born a poor black child.'
Steve Martin was once the biggest stand-up comedian in America, regularly selling out 20,000-seat arenas in the late 1970s.
Roxanne (1987) was Steve Martin's modern retelling of Cyrano de Bergerac, and he performed his own physical comedy stunts.
Tootsie (1982) starred Dustin Hoffman as an actor who disguises himself as a woman to land a soap opera role, earning ten Oscar nominations.
Dustin Hoffman has said he wept when he realized as Tootsie that he wasn't a beautiful woman, understanding how women are judged by appearance.
My Cousin Vinny (1992) won Marisa Tomei the Oscar for Best Supporting Actress in a comedic performance, a rarity for the Academy.
Joe Pesci's comedic timing in My Cousin Vinny was a stark contrast to his intense roles in Goodfellas and Casino.
A Fish Called Wanda (1988) earned John Cleese an Oscar nomination for Best Original Screenplay — unusual for a broad comedy.
Tina Fey created, wrote, and starred in 30 Rock, which ran for seven seasons and won 16 Primetime Emmy Awards.
30 Rock holds the record for the most Emmy nominations for a comedy series in a single year, with 22 nominations in 2009.
Brooklyn Nine-Nine was canceled by Fox in 2018 but saved by NBC within 24 hours after a massive fan campaign on social media.
The Good Place, created by Michael Schur, explored moral philosophy through comedy and was praised for its ambitious storytelling.
Schitt's Creek swept all seven major comedy categories at the 2020 Emmys, the first show ever to do so.
Schitt's Creek was created by Eugene Levy and his son Dan Levy, who also starred together in the series.
Fleabag, created by and starring Phoebe Waller-Bridge, won six Emmy Awards in 2019 including Outstanding Comedy Series.
Phoebe Waller-Bridge originally developed Fleabag as a one-woman stage show at the Edinburgh Festival Fringe in 2013.
Ted Lasso won the Emmy for Outstanding Comedy Series in both 2021 and 2022, making Jason Sudeikis a two-time Best Actor winner.
Ted Lasso was based on a character Jason Sudeikis created for NBC Sports promotional videos about the Premier League.
The Simpsons premiered on December 17, 1989, and is the longest-running American animated series and scripted primetime TV show.
South Park creators Trey Parker and Matt Stone made the pilot episode using paper cutout animation, which took three months to complete.
Modern South Park episodes are typically written and animated in just six days using digital animation.
Family Guy was canceled twice by Fox before fan campaigns and strong DVD sales brought it back.
Bob's Burgers premiered in 2011 and has been praised for its warmth and heart, unusual for an adult animated comedy.
Archer, the animated spy comedy, has run for over 14 seasons and is known for its rapid-fire dialogue and obscure references.
Rick and Morty co-creator Justin Roiland voiced both title characters for the show's first four seasons.
Richard Pryor's Live in Concert (1979) is considered one of the greatest stand-up specials ever recorded and set the template for concert comedy films.
Richard Pryor won five Grammy Awards for his comedy albums and was the first recipient of the Mark Twain Prize for American Humor.
George Carlin's 'Seven Words You Can Never Say on Television' routine led to a Supreme Court case (FCC v. Pacifica Foundation) in 1978.
George Carlin hosted the very first episode of Saturday Night Live on October 11, 1975.
Joan Rivers was a pioneer for women in stand-up comedy and became the first woman to host a late-night talk show with The Late Show in 1986.
Phyllis Diller was one of the first female stand-up comedians to achieve mainstream success, starting her career at age 37 in 1955.
Moms Mabley was one of the first professional female stand-up comedians in the US, performing in front of audiences as early as the 1920s.
Don Rickles, known as 'Mr. Warmth,' built his entire career on insult comedy and was beloved by every comedian he roasted.
Rodney Dangerfield opened his own comedy club, Dangerfield's, in New York City in 1969 to showcase young comedians.
Bob Hope performed for US troops overseas for nearly 50 years, from World War II through the Gulf War.
The Hangover Part II (2011) grossed $586 million worldwide, making it one of the highest-grossing R-rated comedies of all time.
We're the Millers (2013) starred Jason Sudeikis and Jennifer Aniston and grossed $270 million worldwide.
21 Jump Street (2012) reinvented the buddy cop comedy genre and earned $201 million worldwide.
The jump scare at the end of Scary Movie (2000) spoofed The Usual Suspects and became one of the film's most quoted moments.
Scary Movie (2000) launched the modern parody movie trend and grossed $278 million worldwide.
Not Another Teen Movie (2001) parodied every major teen film of the 1990s in one film.
White Chicks (2004) required Shawn and Marlon Wayans to spend seven hours in makeup each day.
Rush Hour (1998) paired Jackie Chan with Chris Tucker and grossed $244 million, launching a successful franchise.
Jackie Chan performed all his own stunts throughout his career, resulting in nearly every bone in his body being broken at some point.
The 'blooper reel' format during end credits — showing actors cracking up during takes — was popularized by Jackie Chan films.
School of Rock (2003) starred Jack Black and was written by Mike White in just three days.
Jack Black co-founded the comedy rock duo Tenacious D with Kyle Gass in 1994.
Nacho Libre (2006) was based on the true story of a Mexican priest who became a professional wrestler to earn money for an orphanage.
Waiting for Guffman (1996) was Christopher Guest's mockumentary about a small-town theater production, improvised almost entirely by the cast.
Best in Show (2000) and A Mighty Wind (2003) continued Christopher Guest's mockumentary style with mostly improvised dialogue.
This Is Spinal Tap (1984) was so convincing as a documentary that many viewers didn't realize it was fictional.
The fake band Spinal Tap from the film actually toured and released real albums, blurring the line between fiction and reality.
Mel Brooks and Carl Reiner were lifelong friends who ate dinner together almost every night for over 50 years until Reiner passed away in 2020.
The phrase 'jumped the shark' originated from a 1977 Happy Days episode and is now used to describe the moment a TV show begins to decline.
Friends aired its final episode on May 6, 2004, drawing 52.5 million viewers, making it the most-watched TV episode of the 2000s decade.
The six main cast members of Friends negotiated together to each earn $1 million per episode in the final seasons.
The Big Bang Theory finale in 2019 drew 23.4 million viewers, and the overall series has earned over $1 billion in syndication revenue.
How I Met Your Mother ran for nine seasons, and its controversial finale divided fans who felt the ending contradicted the show's premise.
Modern Family used the mockumentary format and won the Emmy for Outstanding Comedy Series five years in a row from 2010 to 2014.
Veep earned Julia Louis-Dreyfus six consecutive Emmy Awards for Outstanding Lead Actress in a Comedy Series, a record.
Julia Louis-Dreyfus has won more Emmy Awards for acting than any other performer in television history.
Seinfeld Episodes were famous for not learning lessons — Larry David enforced a 'no hugging, no learning' rule.
The Fresh Prince of Bel-Air (1990-1996) launched Will Smith's acting career and featured one of the most recognizable theme songs in TV history.
Everybody Loves Raymond ran for nine seasons and was based on the real-life experiences of stand-up comedian Ray Romano.
Malcolm in the Middle (2000-2006) was filmed without a laugh track, which was unusual for network sitcoms at the time.
Scrubs (2001-2010) was praised for blending slapstick comedy with genuinely emotional storylines about life and death.
Community (2009-2015) became famous for its genre-bending episodes, including paintball action movies, claymation, and a Ken Burns-style documentary.
The 'Remedial Chaos Theory' episode of Community, which explores alternate timelines, is considered one of the best sitcom episodes of the 2010s.
New Girl (2011-2018) starred Zooey Deschanel and popularized the 'adorkable' comedy trend of the early 2010s.
Silicon Valley (2014-2019) was co-created by Mike Judge and satirized the real tech industry with input from actual Silicon Valley engineers.
What We Do in the Shadows (2019-present) is based on the 2014 film of the same name by Taika Waititi and Jemaine Clement.
Taika Waititi directed Jojo Rabbit (2019), a comedy set during World War II in which he played an imaginary Adolf Hitler, winning the Oscar for Best Adapted Screenplay.
Little Miss Sunshine (2006) was an indie comedy that won two Oscars and launched the careers of Paul Dano and Abigail Breslin.
Juno (2007) earned Diablo Cody the Oscar for Best Original Screenplay and grossed $231 million on a $7.5 million budget.
The Grand Budapest Hotel (2014) was Wes Anderson's most commercially successful film, grossing $172 million worldwide.
Wes Anderson's symmetrical visual style has become so distinctive that it spawned a viral social media trend called 'Accidentally Wes Anderson.'
Home Alone (1990) grossed $476 million worldwide, making it the highest-grossing comedy film at the time.
Macaulay Culkin was just 10 years old when Home Alone made him one of the biggest movie stars in the world.
The Birdcage (1996) starred Robin Williams and Nathan Lane and was an American remake of the French film La Cage aux Folles.
Tootsie (1982) was so difficult to make that Dustin Hoffman and director Sydney Pollack clashed throughout filming.
Bill Hader created and starred in Barry (2018-2023), a dark comedy about a hitman who discovers a passion for acting.
Barry won Bill Hader consecutive Emmy Awards for Outstanding Lead Actor in a Comedy Series in 2018 and 2019.
Atlanta, created by Donald Glover, blended surreal comedy with social commentary and won multiple Emmy Awards.
Donald Glover (Childish Gambino) became the first Black director to win the Emmy for Outstanding Directing for a Comedy Series for Atlanta.
Only Murders in the Building (2021-present) paired Steve Martin and Martin Short with Selena Gomez in a comedy-mystery format.
Steve Martin and Martin Short have been close friends and comedy partners since they met on the set of Three Amigos (1986).
Melissa McCarthy received an Oscar nomination for Best Supporting Actress for Bridesmaids, a rare feat for a comedic role.
Melissa McCarthy's Spy (2015) grossed $235 million worldwide and proved she could carry an action-comedy as a leading lady.
The Heat (2013) paired Sandra Bullock and Melissa McCarthy in a buddy cop comedy that grossed $229 million.
Galaxy Quest (1999) was initially dismissed as a Star Trek parody but is now considered one of the best Star Trek films by fans.
Tim Allen, Sigourney Weaver, and Alan Rickman all took pay cuts to appear in Galaxy Quest because they loved the script.
Ghostbusters: Afterlife (2021) served as a direct sequel to the original 1984 film, ignoring the 2016 reboot.
The Internship (2013) starring Vince Vaughn and Owen Wilson was filmed at Google's actual headquarters.
Night at the Museum (2006) starred Ben Stiller and grossed $574 million worldwide, spawning two sequels.
Ben Stiller has directed several comedies including Reality Bites, Zoolander, Tropic Thunder, and The Secret Life of Walter Mitty.
Meet the Parents (2000) grossed $330 million worldwide and spawned a franchise with two sequels.
Paul Rudd has been called 'the internet's boyfriend' for his everyman charm and was named People's Sexiest Man Alive in 2021.
Paul Rudd has been showing the same clip from the film Mac and Me during every appearance on Conan O'Brien's shows for over 20 years.
Conan O'Brien hosted late-night television for 28 years across NBC and TBS before transitioning to podcasting and a travel show.
The Tonight Show has had only six permanent hosts in its 70+ year history: Steve Allen, Jack Paar, Johnny Carson, Jay Leno, Conan O'Brien, and Jimmy Fallon.
Johnny Carson hosted The Tonight Show for 30 years, and his final episode on May 22, 1992, drew 50 million viewers.
Johnny Carson launched the careers of countless comedians by inviting them to perform stand-up on The Tonight Show.
Whose Line Is It Anyway? popularized improv comedy on television, showing audiences that entire scenes could be created without a script.
The Carol Burnett Show (1967-1978) won 25 Primetime Emmy Awards and is considered one of the greatest variety/comedy shows ever.
I Love Lucy premiered on October 15, 1951, and pioneered the three-camera sitcom format that is still used today.
Lucille Ball was the first woman to run a major television studio, Desilu Productions, which produced Star Trek and Mission: Impossible.
M*A*S*H's finale on February 28, 1983, was watched by 105.9 million viewers, making it the most-watched broadcast in American television history for decades.
Taxi (1978-1983) launched the careers of Danny DeVito, Christopher Lloyd, and Andy Kaufman.
Andy Kaufman deliberately blurred the line between comedy and reality, and many people still debate whether his antics were performances or genuine behavior.
Cheers ran for 11 seasons (1982-1993) and its finale was watched by 80.4 million viewers.
Frasier, the spinoff of Cheers, won the Emmy for Outstanding Comedy Series a record five consecutive times from 1994 to 1998.
The Cosby Show revived the sitcom genre in the 1980s and was the No. 1 rated show on television for five consecutive seasons.
Roseanne (1988-1997) was groundbreaking for depicting a working-class family dealing with real financial struggles in a comedic setting.
Will & Grace (1998-2006, 2017-2020) was one of the first network sitcoms to feature openly gay lead characters.
The IT Crowd, a British sitcom about IT support workers, has a cult following despite running for only four series plus a special.
Fawlty Towers, created by John Cleese, consisted of only 12 episodes across two seasons but is consistently ranked as one of the greatest British comedies.
Blackadder, starring Rowan Atkinson, reinvented itself each season by setting the show in a different historical period.
Mr. Bean, also starring Rowan Atkinson, relied almost entirely on physical comedy with minimal dialogue, making it popular worldwide.
The original British version of The Office ran for only 14 episodes plus a Christmas special but won a Golden Globe for Best Comedy.
Ricky Gervais co-created the original UK Office with Stephen Merchant, and the show inspired remakes in over a dozen countries.
Peep Show (2003-2015) was one of the longest-running sitcoms on Channel 4 and was shot entirely from the characters' point of view.
Ted (2012), directed by Seth MacFarlane, grossed $549 million worldwide, making it the highest-grossing original R-rated comedy.
Seth MacFarlane voiced the character of Ted using a Boston accent inspired by Peter Griffin from Family Guy.
Pitch Perfect (2012) was based on a non-fiction book about collegiate a cappella groups and grossed $115 million on a $17 million budget.
Crazy Rich Asians (2018) was a romantic comedy that grossed $238 million worldwide and was the first major studio film with an all-Asian cast since The Joy Luck Club in 1993.
The Princess Bride (1987) flopped at the box office but became one of the most beloved comedies of all time through home video.
Most of the sword fight between Cary Elwes and Mandy Patinkin in The Princess Bride was performed by the actors themselves after months of training.
Spaceballs (1987) was so quotable that 'May the Schwartz be with you' became a well-known catchphrase.
The phrase 'comedy gold' is often attributed to the writing room at Saturday Night Live, where writers use it to describe a sketch that's guaranteed to get laughs.
Tim Conway was famous for improvising on The Carol Burnett Show, often making co-stars break character and laugh on camera.
The 'Dentist' sketch on The Carol Burnett Show, where Tim Conway improvised while Harvey Korman tried not to laugh, is considered one of the funniest TV moments ever.
In Living Color (1990-1994) launched the careers of Jim Carrey, Jamie Foxx, and the Wayans family.
Jim Carrey was paid $450 per episode on In Living Color — within four years he was earning $20 million per film.
MADtv ran for 14 seasons (1995-2009) on Fox as a competitor to Saturday Night Live.
SCTV (Second City Television) aired from 1976 to 1984 and launched the careers of John Candy, Rick Moranis, Martin Short, and Eugene Levy.
The Kids in the Hall (1989-1995) was a Canadian sketch comedy show where five male comedians played all roles, including female characters.
Mr. Show with Bob and David (1995-1998) starred Bob Odenkirk and David Cross and is considered one of the most influential sketch shows ever.
Bob Odenkirk's later dramatic turn as Saul Goodman in Breaking Bad and Better Call Saul surprised many fans who knew him primarily as a comedy writer.
Christopher Guest, Eugene Levy, and Catherine O'Hara have collaborated on multiple mockumentary films spanning decades.
Harold Ramis co-wrote or directed many classic comedies including Animal House, Caddyshack, Ghostbusters, Groundhog Day, and Analyze This.
Ivan Reitman directed Ghostbusters, Stripes, and Kindergarten Cop — his son Jason Reitman also became a successful director.
The term 'slapstick' comes from the battacchio or slap stick, a prop used in commedia dell'arte to make a loud slapping sound.
Silent film comedians like Chaplin and Keaton influenced nearly every physical comedian who followed, from Jerry Lewis to Jim Carrey.
Harold Lloyd hung from a real clock face 12 stories above the street in Safety Last! (1923), one of the most iconic stunts in film history.
Harold Lloyd lost his right thumb and index finger in a 1919 accident but continued performing his own stunts using a prosthetic glove.
The Marx Brothers made 13 feature films and were known for their anarchic humor, wordplay, and musical performances.
Groucho Marx's painted-on mustache, cigar, and rapid-fire wisecracks made him one of the most imitated comedians of the 20th century.
W.C. Fields was known for his comedic persona as a misanthropic drunk and his famous quote 'Never work with animals or children.'
Jerry Lewis's partnership with Dean Martin produced 16 films in six years before their acrimonious split in 1956.
Jerry Lewis was revered in France, where he was awarded the Legion of Honor for his contributions to comedy and cinema.
Peter Sellers's improvisational skills in the Pink Panther films created the bumbling Inspector Clouseau, one of cinema's most beloved comic characters.
Woody Allen wrote and directed over 50 films during his career, including Annie Hall, which won the Best Picture Oscar in 1978.
Annie Hall (1977) beat Star Wars for the Best Picture Oscar, one of the biggest upsets in Academy Award history.
Bill Murray was offered the role of Forrest Gump before Tom Hanks.
Billy Crystal hosted the Academy Awards nine times, more than any other host.
Whoopi Goldberg hosted the Oscars four times and is one of only 18 people to have achieved EGOT status.
Eddie Izzard is known for performing stand-up comedy in multiple languages, including French and German.
Ali Wong's Netflix special Baby Cobra (2016) became a viral sensation, and she performed it while seven months pregnant.
Hannah Gadsby's Nanette (2018) redefined what a comedy special could be, blending personal trauma with critiques of the comedy form itself.
Bo Burnham's Inside (2021) was filmed entirely in one room during COVID-19 lockdown and won three Emmy Awards.
Bo Burnham started his career posting comedy songs to YouTube at age 16 and became one of the platform's earliest viral stars.
John Mulaney's stand-up special Kid Gorgeous at Radio City (2018) won the Emmy for Outstanding Writing for a Variety Special.
Hasan Minhaj's Patriot Act was the first weekly comedy show hosted by an Indian American on a major network or streaming platform.
Maria Bamford's Lady Dynamite (2016-2017) on Netflix was a semi-autobiographical sitcom praised for its honest portrayal of mental illness in comedy.
Wanda Sykes became the first openly gay Black woman to host a late-night talk show.
The Kentucky Fried Movie (1977) was the first film by the Zucker-Abrahams-Zucker team, who would go on to make Airplane! and The Naked Gun.
Ace Ventura: When Nature Calls (1995) grossed $212 million worldwide, more than the original film.
Tommy Boy (1995) was a commercial success largely due to the chemistry between Chris Farley and David Spade.
Chris Farley's physical comedy on SNL, especially his 'motivational speaker' Matt Foley character, became legendary.
Chris Farley was in talks to voice Shrek before his death in 1997 — Mike Myers eventually took over the role.
Adam McKay transitioned from directing Will Ferrell comedies to directing acclaimed dramas like The Big Short and Vice.
The Big Short (2015) used celebrity cameos to explain complex financial concepts in comedic cutaway scenes.
Horrible Bosses (2011) starred Jason Bateman, Charlie Day, and Jason Sudeikis and grossed $209 million worldwide.
Game Night (2018) was praised as one of the best studio comedies of the 2010s, blending action-thriller tropes with sharp humor.
Free Guy (2021) was Ryan Reynolds's original comedy concept about a video game NPC becoming self-aware, grossing $331 million.
Ryan Reynolds developed his comedic persona through films like Van Wilder, Deadpool, and Free Guy.
Deadpool (2016) was the first R-rated superhero film to gross over $750 million worldwide.
The original test footage for Deadpool was 'leaked' online in 2014, generating so much excitement that Fox greenlit the film.
Spy (2015) was Paul Feig's action comedy starring Melissa McCarthy that earned rave reviews and $235 million worldwide.
Paul Feig directed Bridesmaids, The Heat, Spy, and the 2016 Ghostbusters reboot, becoming known for female-led comedies.
Girls Trip (2017) grossed $140 million worldwide, making Tiffany Haddish a breakout star.
Tiffany Haddish's comedy special Black Mitzvah (2019) won a Grammy Award for Best Comedy Album.
Coming 2 America (2021) was released on Amazon Prime Video, 33 years after the original theatrical release.
The improvised 'I'm in a glass case of emotion' line from Anchorman was one of many Will Ferrell ad-libs that made the final cut.
Seth Rogen and James Franco have appeared together in over ten films and TV shows since Freaks and Geeks.
Forgetting Sarah Marshall (2008) was written by Jason Segel, who insisted on a full-frontal nude scene for authenticity.
Get Him to the Greek (2010) was a spinoff of Forgetting Sarah Marshall featuring Russell Brand's character Aldous Snow.
Popstar: Never Stop Never Stopping (2016) by The Lonely Island was a commercial failure but became a cult comedy hit on streaming.
The Lonely Island (Andy Samberg, Jorma Taccone, Akiva Schaffer) created viral SNL Digital Shorts including 'Lazy Sunday' and 'Dick in a Box.'
Weird Al Yankovic has been parodying popular songs for over 40 years and won five Grammy Awards.
UHF (1989) starring Weird Al Yankovic bombed at the box office but became a cult comedy classic on VHS.
Tenacious D in The Pick of Destiny (2006) was a box office bomb but developed a passionate cult following.
Click (2006) was one of Adam Sandler's highest-grossing films at $237 million worldwide.
Idiocracy (2006) was buried by Fox with minimal marketing but became a cult classic whose satirical predictions are often cited as prophetic.
Mike Judge also created Beavis and Butt-Head, King of the Hill, and Silicon Valley, spanning animation and live-action comedy.
Airplane II: The Sequel (1982) was made without the Zucker brothers or Jim Abrahams, and is often considered inferior to the original.
Police Academy (1984) spawned seven films despite increasingly negative reviews, grossing over $240 million combined.
Coming to America featured Eddie Murphy playing multiple characters including the old Jewish barber Saul, showcasing his makeup transformation skills.
Mrs. Doubtfire won the Academy Award for Best Makeup, recognizing the incredible prosthetics that transformed Robin Williams.
Liar Liar features a famous courtroom scene where Jim Carrey beats himself up, which was largely improvised.
Yes Man (2008) was based on a memoir by Danny Wallace about his real experience of saying 'yes' to everything for a year.
Dumb and Dumber To (2014) was made 20 years after the original, reuniting Jim Carrey and Jeff Daniels.
Jeff Daniels, primarily known as a dramatic actor, delivered a legendary comedic performance in Dumb and Dumber.
The 'most annoying sound in the world' scene in Dumb and Dumber was improvised by Jim Carrey.
Bill Murray reportedly called Chevy Chase 'medium talent' during a heated backstage argument before Murray's first SNL appearance.
National Lampoon's Christmas Vacation (1989) is the third film in the Vacation franchise and has become a staple of holiday viewing.
Clark Griswold's rant about his boss in Christmas Vacation was partly improvised by Chevy Chase.
The Waterboy (1998) grossed $185 million worldwide, making it Adam Sandler's second-highest-grossing film at the time.
Big Daddy (1999) grossed $234 million and showed Adam Sandler could blend comedy with more sentimental storytelling.
You Don't Mess with the Zohan (2008) was co-written by Adam Sandler, Robert Smigel, and Judd Apatow.
Grown Ups (2010) reunited SNL alumni Adam Sandler, Chris Rock, David Spade, Rob Schneider, and Kevin James.
Murder Mystery (2019) starring Adam Sandler and Jennifer Aniston was watched by 73 million Netflix accounts in its first four weeks.
Hustle (2022) earned Adam Sandler praise for a dramatic-comedy role as a basketball scout, continuing his shift toward more serious films.
The term 'straight man' in comedy refers to the serious character who sets up jokes for the comedian, like Bud Abbott to Lou Costello.
Improv comedy's 'yes, and...' principle — accepting and building on whatever your scene partner says — has been adopted as a business and leadership technique.
The Groundlings theater in Los Angeles was founded in 1974 and has been a launching pad for SNL performers for decades.
UCB's New York location at 307 West 26th Street in Chelsea was a mecca for improv comedy from 2003 until it closed in 2020.
Del Close, a legendary improv teacher at Second City and ImprovOlympic, is credited with developing the long-form improv format known as 'The Harold.'
Tina Fey's memoir Bossypants (2011) was one of the best-selling comedy books of the decade.
Amy Poehler's memoir Yes Please (2014) referenced her improv training and its influence on her comedy philosophy.
Mindy Kaling wrote for and starred in The Office before creating The Mindy Project (2012-2017).
The Marvelous Mrs. Maisel (2017-2023) won eight Emmy Awards in its first season, including Outstanding Comedy Series.
Reservation Dogs (2021-2023) was the first TV series to feature an all-Indigenous writers' room, blending comedy with Native American storytelling.
Abbott Elementary, created by and starring Quinta Brunson, premiered in 2021 and revived the network sitcom format to critical acclaim.
Quinta Brunson was a BuzzFeed video creator before becoming one of the most celebrated comedy showrunners on television.
Hacks (2021-present) stars Jean Smart as a legendary Las Vegas comedian and has won multiple Emmy Awards.
The Bear (2022-present) blends comedy with high-pressure kitchen drama and swept the comedy categories at the 2023 Emmys.
Tommy Boy's 'fat guy in a little coat' scene was improvised by Chris Farley, who used to perform the bit backstage at SNL.
Beverly Hills Ninja (1997) was one of Chris Farley's last films before his death on December 18, 1997.
Phil Hartman, one of SNL's most versatile cast members, was known as 'The Glue' because he could make any sketch work.
Gilda Radner was one of the original SNL cast members and created beloved characters like Roseanne Roseannadanna and Emily Litella.
National Lampoon magazine, founded in 1970, spawned films, radio shows, and a comedy empire that influenced humor for decades.
The comedy roast tradition was popularized by the Friars Club in New York, which has been roasting celebrities since the early 1900s.
Comedy Central's roasts became a cultural phenomenon in the 2000s, with targets including Pamela Anderson, Donald Trump, and Justin Bieber.
The Edinburgh Festival Fringe is the world's largest arts festival and has launched the careers of many stand-up comedians.
Dry Bar Comedy, which features clean stand-up comedy, has garnered billions of views on YouTube.
Netflix's investment in stand-up comedy specials beginning around 2015 reinvigorated the genre and made it accessible to global audiences.
Dave Chappelle's Netflix deal in 2016 was reportedly worth $60 million for three specials.
Jerry Seinfeld's Comedians in Cars Getting Coffee ran for 11 seasons, first on Crackle and then Netflix.
The 'Aristocrats' joke is a notoriously filthy joke that comedians use as a test of improvisational skill — it was the subject of a 2005 documentary.
Mitch Hedberg's one-liner style and deadpan delivery made him a cult comedy legend before his death in 2005 at age 37.
Steven Wright's deadpan delivery and absurdist one-liners won him a cult following and an Academy Award for his short film.
Brian Regan is often cited by other comedians as one of the funniest stand-up comics working, despite maintaining a clean act.
Norm Macdonald was fired from SNL's Weekend Update in 1998 reportedly because an NBC executive didn't find him funny.
Norm Macdonald's 'moth' joke on Conan O'Brien's show is widely regarded as one of the greatest late-night comedy moments.
Sarah Silverman's provocative comedy style pushed boundaries, and her show The Sarah Silverman Program ran for three seasons on Comedy Central.
Conan O'Brien's brief, contentious stint as Tonight Show host in 2010 lasted only seven months before he was replaced by Jay Leno.
Craig Ferguson hosted The Late Late Show from 2005 to 2014 and was known for his off-the-cuff, unscripted opening monologues.
John Oliver's Last Week Tonight on HBO debuted in 2014 and pioneered the long-form comedic deep dive into serious topics.
The Daily Show with Jon Stewart helped shape a generation's approach to news and politics through comedy.
Stephen Colbert's character on The Colbert Report was a parody of conservative pundits, particularly Bill O'Reilly.
Between Two Ferns with Zach Galifianakis was a viral Funny or Die web series known for awkward celebrity interviews.
Key and Peele's 'Substitute Teacher' sketch featuring the character Mr. Garvey has been viewed over 200 million times on YouTube.
Drunk History, which began as a web series in 2007, ran for six seasons on Comedy Central, featuring comedians narrating historical events while intoxicated.
The term 'sitcom' is short for 'situational comedy' and was first used in the 1950s to describe shows like I Love Lucy.
The laugh track was invented by sound engineer Charley Douglass in the 1950s using a device called the 'laff box.'
Multi-camera sitcoms like Friends and The Big Bang Theory are filmed in front of live studio audiences, while single-camera shows like The Office are not.
The phrase 'breaking the fourth wall' refers to a character acknowledging the audience directly, a technique used in comedies from Ferris Bueller to Fleabag.
Some Like It Hot ends with a famous final line — 'Well, nobody's perfect' — which was meant as a placeholder but worked so well it stayed.
My Big Fat Greek Wedding (2002) was made for just $5 million and grossed $368 million worldwide, the highest-grossing romantic comedy ever at the time.
The Full Monty (1997) was a British comedy about unemployed steelworkers who become strippers that became one of the highest-grossing films in UK history.
Four Weddings and a Funeral (1994) launched Hugh Grant's international career and grossed $245 million worldwide.
Notting Hill (1999) paired Hugh Grant with Julia Roberts and grossed $363 million, making it the highest-grossing British film at the time.
Love Actually (2003) has become one of the most popular Christmas films despite mixed reviews on its initial release.
Borat Subsequent Moviefilm (2020) was secretly filmed during COVID-19 and released on Amazon Prime Video.
Bruno (2009) was Sacha Baron Cohen's follow-up to Borat and featured similarly uncomfortable interactions with real people.
Da Ali G Show, which introduced Borat and Bruno, premiered on Channel 4 in the UK in 2000.
